results.possibleIdentity: Gary J. Griffith was a physicist and technical expert active in the late 1960s and 1970s, primarily associated with the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) and the interdisciplinary organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He studied physics at the University of California, Berkeley, where he was a member of the class of 1963. During his tenure at NASA, particularly at the Ames Research Center near Berkeley, his work focused on technical fields such as optics, light, lasers, holography, and photography. Griffith was part of the early wave of engineers and scientists who participated in E.A.T., a nonprofit organization founded to foster collaborations between the technical and artistic communities. This association often involved providing technical expertise in optics and laser technology to artists exploring new media like holography. Later in his career, he became a prominent author and consultant in the fields of metrology and quality control, writing influential texts such as "The Quality Technician's Handbook" and "Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing: Applications and Inspection," which include detailed applications of light sensors, fiber optics, and laser phenomena.
